metadata.title: Disperse and preserve the perverse: computing how hip-hop censorship changed popular music genres in China

abstract:
How do political interventions reshape genre boundaries? Previous studies on genres only tangentially touch on this question as they mostly focus on the artistic, economic, or critical consequences of genre spanning. This paper fills in this gap by exploring the impact of music censorship on the censored genre and other related genres. Using an original dataset of 53,364 songs released on a Chinese online music platform, I study how Hip-Hop censorship in China in 2018 impacted Hip-Hop as well as Pop, Rock, and Folk songs in terms of how they sound and what topics are engaged in the lyrics. I propose a novel, computational approach to measure sound similarities between songs by using Music Information Retrieval (MIR) algorithms which synthesize audio signal processing and neural networks. I also measure the change of topic prevalence in song lyrics by using topic models supplemented with a dictionary approach. I found that post-censorship Hip-Hop songs sound significantly different from pre-censorship ones, with a bigger impact on the high-profile songs. Moreover, Rock, as a close genre to Hip-Hop, became more "Hip-Hoppy", while Pop, a mixed category that reflects trending genres, became less "Hip-Hoppy"; the impact on these two genres is more salient among their low-profile songs. Folk, a genre distanced from Hip-Hop, remained generally untouched. The censorship also made Hip-Hop musicians engage less with topics related to violence and deviant behaviors but more with sexual terms, albeit in a covered form and not necessarily related to sexual conduct per se. The findings suggest a dispersion model in explaining the outcome of political interventions in genres, where stylistic conventions of the censored genre are dispersed from salient works of that genre to less influential ones as well as to adjacent genres.

A core tenet of cultural sociology is that artistic creation is constrained by genre conventions and boundaries, based on which the producers, consumers, and intermediaries share mutual expectations on what makes an artistic style distinctive from the others

paragraphs: spanning as self-motivated practices of the artists.

But what if the artists are pressured to cross boundaries? This can be seen in cases of political intervention affecting art worlds through censorship, where specific genres are suppressed by the state due to concerns that the art may evoke potential political unrest or moral panic. Existing studies have provided a rich record of how the creation of specific artworks can be forbidden, altered, or appropriated by the state or political groups (e.g., Beisel, 1993;De Kloet, 2010;Hall, 2017;Johnson, 2016;Lena, 2012;Zeng and Sparks, 2019). Yet a more systematic investigation on how political interventions reshape genre boundaries is relatively scarce in the study of genres. When a genre is censored, do the artists of the genre start to produce a different style of art? Does the whole genre simply "disappear" when confronted with genre-wide censorship? Are the artists of other related genres affected in their artistic creativity too?

The investigation of these questions is especially needed when one observes that the censorship of genre may yield perplexing outcomes, as in the case of the Hip-Hop censorship in China in 2018. The Hip-Hop censorship was announced on January 19, 2018 in a press conference by one of the major cultural regulation institutions at the time, the State Administration of Press, Publication, Radio, Film, and Television of China (SAPPRFT), 1 after Hip-Hop music went viral in China in 2017 due to a successful reality TV show, The Rap of China (Amar, 2018;Zhang, 2020). The censorship declared that Hip-Hop musicians "should not be featured" in mainstream media as they are "not aligned with the party" and their "morality is not noble" (Quackenbush and Chen, 2018). The resulting consequences of the censorship were puzzling. On the one hand, Hip-Hop musicians were generally banned from publicly performing at large venues and lost other economic opportunities right after the censorship, suggesting a serious crackdown on the genre. Some of them were not allowed to release songs publicly in mainland China since then. On the other hand, many songs labeled as Hip-Hop were still being produced and circulated publicly, and, perhaps counterintuitively, more Hip-Hop songs were produced and posted on online music platforms in the year after the censorship than the year before.

So, does censorship of a genre work at all and, if it does, how? In this study, I propose a dispersion model to characterize how political interventions reshape genres. Since the state is usually concerned about the political or moral disquiet that would involve a large public (Baranovitch, 2003;Becker, 1982), I speculate that the artworks of the censored genre that are widely distributed may be under severe scrutiny after the censorship, while those that are not widely distributed, and hence less influential, are less scrutinized. Therefore, the stylistic conventions of the censored genre, especially those that are explicitly problematic to the state, may be dropped by the high-profile post-censorship works of that genre as to be accepted by the state. However, these conventions may still be sustained by the low-profile works of the censored genre and picked up by other genres as well. In other words, problematic elements are dispersed from the popular works of the censored genre to lesser known works of that genre or to other related genres.

To test the validity of the dispersion model, I use the Hip-Hop censorship in China in 2018 as a case study, focusing on how the censorship changed the sound and the lyrics of the songs released after the censorship compared to those before. I collected an original dataset of 53,364 Chinese-language songs of four genres (Hip-Hop, Pop, Rock, and Folk) released between January 1, 2015 and December 31, 2018, from one of the most popular online music platforms in mainland China, including their audio files, lyrics, and other basic information. To compare the sound of the songs released before and after the censorship, I take advantage of the computational tools that are widely used in Music Information Retrieval (MIR) to extract acoustic features of the music and to build neural-network-based classifiers to measure the musical similarities of the songs within and across genres. Additionally, I use topic models and a dictionary approach to check how topics of the lyrics changed after the censorship and how the use of sensitive terms, such as curse words, are incorporated in the lyrics.

The findings generally support the proposal of the dispersion model. First and foremost, the Hip-Hop songs released after the censorship sound significantly different from those before. Particularly, high-profile Hip-Hop songs released after the censorship sound significantly less like the songs that were censored, while this shift was insignificant among low-profile Hip-Hop songs. Although Folk, a genre that is artistically distanced from Hip-Hop, was largely unaffected by the censorship, other genres were impacted: Rock, as a close genre to Hip-Hop, took advantage of the censorship by becoming more "Hip-Hoppy", whereas Pop, a hodgepodge category that reflects whatever genres are trending, mirrored the impact on Hip-Hop and became less "Hip-Hoppy". The effect on Rock and Pop is most salient among their low-profile songs, indicating that the stylistic conventions of other genres are not easily changed. Moreover, the findings from the exploration of lyrics suggest that while Hip-Hop songs started to engage less with sensitive topics such as violence and deviant behaviors, sex terms became more prevalent among Hip-Hop songs, although in a disguised manner to avoid censorship detection and often not specifically related to sexual conduct per se. This paper will be organized as follows. First, I will review the existing literature on the study of genres and the scholarship of political interventions in art, pointing out the gap in the literature and specifying the dispersion model. I will then offer some background regarding the history of Hip-Hop music and its censorship in China, where I will pay particular attention to the ambiguity of the censorship. Subsequently, I will elaborate on the data that I collected for the study, followed by the explanation of how I used computational tools, such as MIR algorithms and topic models, to construct formal measurements to examine the change of musical features and topical focus of songs. I will discuss the implications of this study after presenting the findings.

paragraphs: Social scientists have long been interested in how categories establish social and symbolic boundaries, and thereby constitute the 1 In 2018, the institution was restructured and renamed the National Radio and Television Administration (NRTA). identity of actors (DiMaggio, 1997;Douglas, 1986;Durand and Paolella, 2013;Lamont and Molnár, 2002;Mohr and Duquenne, 1997;Zerubavel, 1993). Some pay specific attention to the categories of cultural objects, or artistic genres (Alacovska and O'Brien, 2021;DiMaggio, 1987;Hesmondhalgh, 2005;Hsu, 2006;Lena, 2012;Lena and Peterson, 2008;Negus, 1998;van Venrooij and Schmutz, 2018). These studies on genres, by almost common consent, contend that genres are not defined by specific symbolic or formal features of the cultural objects, nor are they rigidly paired with specific social groups. Rather, the boundary of a genre is constantly drawn and blurred in response to the corresponding condition of social structure and the dynamics of societal change (Bourdieu, 1996;Lena, 2006;Roy, 2002). For example, focusing on the classification of popular music, Lena and Peterson argue that genres are "systems of orientations, expectations, and conventions that bind together an industry, performers, critics, and fans in making what they identify as a distinctive sort of music" (Lena and Peterson, 2008:698). This indicates that genres are not fixed entities but rather classifying apparatuses for making social distinctions. What matters in defining a genre and its boundaries is that there is some degree of consensus shared among social actors that the genre in question is distinctive vis-à-vis other genres, the differences between which are not merely rooted in the artistic traits of cultural objects but also in the social or artistic identity they represent (Lena, 2012;Roy, 2004).

What social factors, then, contribute to defining genres, and how do these social factors shape genre boundaries? The existing literature mostly highlights the economic or artistic considerations. On the one hand, once stablished, a genre tends to reinforce itself as the producers usually conform to the genre conventions in order to signal an unequivocal identity. Straying away from genre conventions will evoke confusion about the identity of the cultural object and its producer and, consequently, will likely to be penalized by consumers and critics (Bielby and Bielby, 1999;Hsu, 2006;Zuckerman, 1999). On the other hand, the boundary between genres can sometimes be crossed, blended, or eroded. Mixing genres can demonstrate the ingenuity and versatility of the producer (Pinheiro and Dowd, 2009;Rao et al., 2005), or it may be a result of technological developments or legal changes (Griswold, 1981;Katz, 2010). The competing views notwithstanding, both sides of this line of inquiry foreground the concern over artistic pursuit, market performance, and critical appraisal as the primary rationales behind whether boundaries are consolidated or diminished.

Yet despite a few important efforts (e.g., Corse, 1995;Lena, 2012), this body of literature generally sheds little light on how political interventions, such as state censorship, can forcefully change genre boundaries. This does not mean that politics is irrelevant on this matter; in fact, many key studies on politics and culture suggest that politics is pivotal in shaping, defining, and even establishing artistic genres (Berezin, 1994;Binder, 1993;Pescosolido, Grauerholz, and Milkie, 1997;Santoro, 2010;Stamatov, 2002). Particularly, the state usually plays a critical role in the cultural production of specific genres of their interest or concern, intervening and controlling the way cultural products are produced especially when the political authority or social order is challenged by the artists and their artworks (Alexander, 1996;Becker, 1982;Lena, 2012). Gleaning from the stories recorded in this line of research, I identify three outcomes in the existing literature that illustrate the effect of state intervention in cultural production of artistic genres. The first outcome is suppression, meaning that the cultural products of specific types are suppressed from being (re-)produced and circulated by the state (Hall, 2017;Johnson, 2016). In this case, the products in question are taken down from the open market, the same type of products are prohibited from being produced and publicly disseminated, and the producers are admonished, silenced, or even incarcerated. Concession, the second outcome, usually involves negotiations between the artists and the state (De Kloet, 2010;Zeng and Sparks, 2019). Consequently, the state does not necessarily cut off the production of the artwork in question, yet its eventual form is contingent upon the outcome of negotiations and mutual compromises. The third outcome is appropriation, observed in situations where the state actively participates in the production of certain artworks. This is best demonstrated by art propaganda, such as "Chinese model opera", in which specific art forms are devised to serve political interests (Clark, Pang, and Tsai, 2016). These three outcomes are neither exhaustive nor mutually exclusive, yet they represent the major consequences of state intervention in cultural production that have been identified by most academic studies of the subject.

While these three outcomes are derived from insightful observations, they are inadequate to fully explain how political intervention shapes genre boundaries especially when the entire genre is under suspicion, which is not unprecedented (De Kloet,. 2010;Lipsitz, 1998), since they only focus on specific artworks or artworks by specific producers. It is neither probable nor practical to believe that the entire genre, in which a multitude of artworks coexist, is suppressed, compromised, or appropriated in the same way. There is also little empirical evidence suggesting that is the case. In fact, the chief purpose of the state intervention is usually to avoid "undesirable public activities" (Becker, 1982:187), including public protest or moral panic, so what really matters to the state is to "disperse" the problematic conventions of the genre, such as the promotion of deviant behaviors or political activism, out of the high profile works. As long as these genre conventions become less salient features of the genre and do not pose a threat to the authority and public tranquility, the state will be less concerned (Baranovitch, 2003;Becker, 1982).

I therefore propose a dispersion model that can better grasp the effects of the political intervention in artistic genres. The dispersion model suggests that despite labeling themselves with the same genre name, the artworks of the targeted genre after the intervention, in general, deviate from the pre-intervention conventions by either becoming less like the artworks before or using covering techniques to veil formal elements that are explicitly problematic to the political forces. In music, specifically, this could mean that the songs of the targeted genre after the censorship might sound different from those before, or they change problematic words in lyrics to a less explicit form. In either case, the formal elements that are typically associated with the targeted genre before the censorship are dispersed from and become less salient in the targeted genre post-censorship.

What does such a dispersion model imply in terms of the impact of political intervention on genre boundaries and how to operationalize the model? I suggest taking consideration of the following two aspects of genre. First, since the genre is the subject of the study, an extensive collection of the artworks associated with the genre before and after the intervention needs to be assembled for analysis. The study should take into account as extensive a range of artworks as possible from within the targeted genre, including artworks that are well-received as epitomizing the genre as well as those that are less central but still identified as within the genre.

Although the high-profile artworks of a genre are often widely distributed, recognized, and considered as the genre ideals, the lowprofile works are no less important in carving the genre boundary by virtue of assigning themselves to the genre. In fact, these marginal works and their producers play a key role in defining what the genre is (or is not), and they sometimes become the originators that reshape and redefine the genre (Bourdieu, 1996). Despite their importance in shaping genre boundaries, the low-profile works are usually of less interest to the state as the state is primarily worried about the impact of the artworks reaching larger audiences, and hence the production and consumption of problematic artworks in a limited circle can often be tolerated (Becker, 1982). This scenario is indeed demonstrable in the case of Hip-Hop censorship in China, where many censored songs were actually released long before the censorship and remained on the online platforms before they brought public attention. Therefore, I speculate that after the political intervention, the subsequently released artworks of the targeted genre that are nevertheless widely circulated would be those dropping the formal elements that might raise concern of the state since they would be enjoined from wide circulation otherwise. In other words, the high-profile artworks within the genre post-intervention will be less similar in their form to those high-profile works prior to the intervention. On the other hand, the low-profile artworks after the intervention will be less affected since they are not of primary interest to the state. In this sense, the genre's boundary becomes blurred as conventions are dispersed from the high-profile artworks, causing the distinctiveness of the genre to become destabilized.

Second, and perhaps more importantly, since different genres are situated in relation to one another and thus the changing boundaries of one genre may imply a corresponding change to others, it is relevant to similarly assess the impact of the political intervention on the genres adjacent to the targeted one. Genres can be considered closely related when they have affinitive stylistic conventions, compete for similar audiences, and share institutional resources such as label companies and venues. Closer genres tend to be used in tandem in the classification of cultural objects (van Venrooij and Schmutz, 2018), and they are also inclined to borrow stylistic elements from each other (Rao et al., 2005;Ruef and Patterson, 2009). Therefore, it is reasonable to suspect that the genres close to the targeted genre may absorb conventions that are dispersed from the targeted genre and thus preserve these conventions in spite of the state's censorship efforts. In this case, the boundary between the targeted genre and a closely related genre would become blurred or altered as a result of the forceful dispersion of artistic conventions following the political intervention.

One important caveat worth mentioning here is about a specific category in popular music, Pop, a common tag that is widely used especially in the popular music charts and distribution channels to denote a particular stream of work that targets a wide range of listeners instead of a particular group of fans. Because Pop is usually comprised of "a succession of hits that are marginally different" and "songs derived from genres that are popular at the moment" (Lena and Peterson, 2008:700), it is usually not considered as a genre itself but a variation upon the current set of trending genres. Therefore, Pop should, to some extent, share the stylistic conventions of the trending genre, and, consequently, it should also experience a similar dispersion from the political intervention in parallel with the trending genre that becomes the target of the censorship.

To test the validity of the dispersion model, I use the 2018 Hip-Hop censorship in China as an empirical case. In the next section, I will provide background on the censorship and elaborate upon why this case provides an ideal site for investigating the impact of political intervention on artistic genres.

This does not mean that politics is irrelevant on this matter; in fact, many key studies on politics and culture suggest that politics is pivotal in shaping, defining, and even establishing artistic genres (Berezin, 1994;Binder, 1993;Pescosolido, Grauerholz, and Milkie, 1997;Santoro, 2010;Stamatov, 2002). Particularly, the state usually plays a critical role in the cultural production of specific genres of their interest or concern, intervening and controlling the way cultural products are produced especially when the political authority or social order is challenged by the artists and their artworks (Alexander, 1996;Becker, 1982;Lena, 2012). Gleaning from the stories recorded in this line of research, I identify three outcomes in the existing literature that illustrate the effect of state intervention in cultural production of artistic genres. The first outcome is suppression, meaning that the cultural products of specific types are suppressed from being (re-)produced and circulated by the state (Hall, 2017;Johnson, 2016). In this case, the products in question are taken down from the open market, the same type of products are prohibited from being produced and publicly disseminated, and the producers are admonished, silenced, or even incarcerated. Concession, the second outcome, usually involves negotiations between the artists and the state (De Kloet, 2010;Zeng and Sparks, 2019). Consequently, the state does not necessarily cut off the production of the artwork in question, yet its eventual form is contingent upon the outcome of negotiations and mutual compromises. The third outcome is appropriation, observed in situations where the state actively participates in the production of certain artworks. This is best demonstrated by art propaganda, such as "Chinese model opera", in which specific art forms are devised to serve political interests (Clark, Pang, and Tsai, 2016). These three outcomes are neither exhaustive nor mutually exclusive, yet they represent the major consequences of state intervention in cultural production that have been identified by most academic studies of the subject.

While these three outcomes are derived from insightful observations, they are inadequate to fully explain how political intervention shapes genre boundaries especially when the entire genre is under suspicion, which is not unprecedented (De Kloet,. 2010;Lipsitz, 1998), since they only focus on specific artworks or artworks by specific producers. It is neither probable nor practical to believe that the entire genre, in which a multitude of artworks coexist, is suppressed, compromised, or appropriated in the same way. There is also little empirical evidence suggesting that is the case. In fact, the chief purpose of the state intervention is usually to avoid "undesirable public activities" (Becker, 1982:187), including public protest or moral panic, so what really matters to the state is to "disperse" the problematic conventions of the genre, such as the promotion of deviant behaviors or political activism, out of the high profile works. As long as these genre conventions become less salient features of the genre and do not pose a threat to the authority and public tranquility, the state will be less concerned (Baranovitch, 2003;Becker, 1982).

I therefore propose a dispersion model that can better grasp the effects of the political intervention in artistic genres. The dispersion model suggests that despite labeling themselves with the same genre name, the artworks of the targeted genre after the intervention, in general, deviate from the pre-intervention conventions by either becoming less like the artworks before or using covering techniques to veil formal elements that are explicitly problematic to the political forces. In music, specifically, this could mean that the songs of the targeted genre after the censorship might sound different from those before, or they change problematic words in lyrics to a less explicit form. In either case, the formal elements that are typically associated with the targeted genre before the censorship are dispersed from and become less salient in the targeted genre post-censorship.

What does such a dispersion model imply in terms of the impact of political intervention on genre boundaries and how to operationalize the model? I suggest taking consideration of the following two aspects of genre. First, since the genre is the subject of the study, an extensive collection of the artworks associated with the genre before and after the intervention needs to be assembled for analysis. The study should take into account as extensive a range of artworks as possible from within the targeted genre, including artworks that are well-received as epitomizing the genre as well as those that are less central but still identified as within the genre.

Although the high-profile artworks of a genre are often widely distributed, recognized, and considered as the genre ideals, the lowprofile works are no less important in carving the genre boundary by virtue of assigning themselves to the genre. In fact, these marginal works and their producers play a key role in defining what the genre is (or is not), and they sometimes become the originators that reshape and redefine the genre (Bourdieu, 1996). Despite their importance in shaping genre boundaries, the low-profile works are usually of less interest to the state as the state is primarily worried about the impact of the artworks reaching larger audiences, and hence the production and consumption of problematic artworks in a limited circle can often be tolerated (Becker, 1982). This scenario is indeed demonstrable in the case of Hip-Hop censorship in China, where many censored songs were actually released long before the censorship and remained on the online platforms before they brought public attention. Therefore, I speculate that after the political intervention, the subsequently released artworks of the targeted genre that are nevertheless widely circulated would be those dropping the formal elements that might raise concern of the state since they would be enjoined from wide circulation otherwise. In other words, the high-profile artworks within the genre post-intervention will be less similar in their form to those high-profile works prior to the intervention. On the other hand, the low-profile artworks after the intervention will be less affected since they are not of primary interest to the state. In this sense, the genre's boundary becomes blurred as conventions are dispersed from the high-profile artworks, causing the distinctiveness of the genre to become destabilized.

Second, and perhaps more importantly, since different genres are situated in relation to one another and thus the changing boundaries of one genre may imply a corresponding change to others, it is relevant to similarly assess the impact of the political intervention on the genres adjacent to the targeted one. Genres can be considered closely related when they have affinitive stylistic conventions, compete for similar audiences, and share institutional resources such as label companies and venues. Closer genres tend to be used in tandem in the classification of cultural objects (van Venrooij and Schmutz, 2018), and they are also inclined to borrow stylistic elements from each other (Rao et al., 2005;Ruef and Patterson, 2009). Therefore, it is reasonable to suspect that the genres close to the targeted genre may absorb conventions that are dispersed from the targeted genre and thus preserve these conventions in spite of the state's censorship efforts. In this case, the boundary between the targeted genre and a closely related genre would become blurred or altered as a result of the forceful dispersion of artistic conventions following the political intervention.

One important caveat worth mentioning here is about a specific category in popular music, Pop, a common tag that is widely used especially in the popular music charts and distribution channels to denote a particular stream of work that targets a wide range of listeners instead of a particular group of fans. Because Pop is usually comprised of "a succession of hits that are marginally different" and "songs derived from genres that are popular at the moment" (Lena and Peterson, 2008:700), it is usually not considered as a genre itself but a variation upon the current set of trending genres. Therefore, Pop should, to some extent, share the stylistic conventions of the trending genre, and, consequently, it should also experience a similar dispersion from the political intervention in parallel with the trending genre that becomes the target of the censorship.

To test the validity of the dispersion model, I use the 2018 Hip-Hop censorship in China as an empirical case. In the next section, I will provide background on the censorship and elaborate upon why this case provides an ideal site for investigating the impact of political intervention on artistic genres.

sections.paper_id: Nie - 2021 - Disperse and preserve the perverse computing how hip-hop censorship changed popular music genres in
paragraphs: Music has long been considered part of state-building in the history of China as different forms of music are believed to be linked with specific kinds of morality and values that can either enhance or deteriorate the state (Brindley, 2012). This is also true for China today, where the ruling Communist Party of China (hereafter CPC) has been suspicious of musical styles, typically originating from the West, that promote values that deviate from the social norms of the socialist society. Although the hostility towards Western popular music plunged since the neo-liberalization starting in the late 1970s, musical genres adopted from Western subcultures still continue to be under heavy government scrutiny (Baranovitch,. 2003).

Hip-Hop music is one such genre with an explicit American origin, derived from the Black community in the Bronx, New York with stylistic elements including sampling, rapping, and scratching with turntables (Chang, 2007;Ewoodzie, 2017). The music was introduced to mainland China by Westerners in the 1990s (Sullivan and Zhao, 2021). Hip-Hop musicians in China generally stayed "underground", performing in pubs and clubs for a restricted audience and circulating their works within a limited scope. They believe that Hip-Hop music is an authentic form of expression through which they are able to manifest themselves or, in their own terms, to be "real" (Zhang, 2020). Hardly resonating with the Black identity and the street culture of their American counterparts, they take on themes that speak more directly to their struggle and aspiration of living in the city, either illustrating an affluent lifestyle full of money, sex, and even drugs or expressing their resistance against the political and educational system (Amar, 2018;Zhang, 2020). This earned them little chance to be attracted by Chinese mainstream media, resulting in limited resources to maintain their existence. Many Hip-Hop musicians even relied on Rock labels to release their music and, therefore, were considered part of the Rock culture (De Kloet, 2010). This historical overlap of music labels affirms the affinity between Rock and Hip-Hop in China.

Chinese Hip-Hop musicians have become more exposed to mainstream media recently, however, due to CPC's increasing emphasis on the cultural industries as an important economic contributor (Keane, 2016). The first appearance of an erstwhile underground Hip-Hop musician in the top mainstream media outlet happened in 2014 when Chengdu rapper Fat Shady (谢帝) sang Daddy ain't go to work tomorrow (老子明天不上班) on a music competition program Sing My Song (中国好歌曲) broadcasted by China Central Television (CCTV), which is the official state television station and the mouthpiece of the Party (Amar, 2018). Nevertheless, Hip-Hop musicians still found it difficult to balance between their musical authenticity, including their lyrics' attention to sensitive topics, and profitability, which necessitates conforming to the state. The authority also periodically banned specific songs that were considered problematic. For example, in August 2015, the Ministry of Culturefoot_0 announced a blacklist of 120 songs that "contain content that promotes sex, violence or crime, or harms public morality", a vast majority of which were from underground Hip-Hop musicians (Hu, 2015). The music streaming platforms in China were asked to pull down the songs permanently.

But Hip-Hop music soon enjoyed an unexpected success in Chinese mainstream media when the first season of The Rap of China was broadcasted in 2017. A reality-show-style music competition produced by iQiyi, a Beijing-based online video platform that has over 500 million monthly active users, The Rap of China brought together underground Hip-Hop musicians and mainstream pop singers, setting the stage for their battle over the "realness" of Hip-Hop and dramatizing the tension between mainstream and underground figures (Zhang, 2020;Zou, 2019). The program started off in June with almost no commercial sponsorship due to concerns over its reception but ended up with 2.7 billion total views in the course of three months, nailing the highest number for a Chinese online program of all time (Flew, Ryan, and Su, 2019). The two co-winners of the competition, GAI and PG One, were already prominent figures in the underground scene in Chongqing and Xi'an, respectively. They and their underground peers ascended to national fame after the show, which brought them considerable commercial contracts and access to mainstream media that they had never had before.

The elevation of the underground Hip-Hop musicians, however, also brought trouble to the artists. Later that year as Christmas approached, a 2015 song Christmas Eve (圣诞夜) by the Xi'an Hip-Hop group HHH (红花会), in which PG One was a key member, resurfaced online and became widely distributed. Despite the fact that the song was written and performed as a group, PG One became the target of a wave of harsh criticism from the CPC-led political organizations and state media, including the Central Committee of the Communist Youth League of China and CPC's mouthpiece People's Daily, who condemned the singer for having promoted drugs, obscenity, and misogyny, along with other illegal behaviors (CGTN, 2018). Below is an excerpt of the song lyrics that illustrate the content of the criticism quite explicitly:foot_1

Despite the fact that the song was written and performed as a group, PG One became the target of a wave of harsh criticism from the CPC-led political organizations and state media, including the Central Committee of the Communist Youth League of China and CPC's mouthpiece People's Daily, who condemned the singer for having promoted drugs, obscenity, and misogyny, along with other illegal behaviors (CGTN, 2018). sections.preview_head: Music has long been considered part of state-building in the history of China as different forms of music are believed to be linked with specific kinds of morality and values that can either enhance or deteriorate the state (Brindley, 2012). This is also true for China today, where the ruling Communist Party of China (hereafter CPC) has been suspicious of musical styles, typically originating from the West, that promote values that deviate from the social norms of the socialist society. Although the hostility towards Western popular music plunged since the neo-liberalization starting in the late 1970s, musical genres adopted from Western subcultures still continue to be under heavy government scrutiny (Baranovitch,. 2003).

paragraphs: Riding on her shoulders, smoking up the fuel, singing jingle bells She says I'm too strong, my dazzling masculinity is like King Kong she loves PG One soon made a public apology on January 4, 2018, in which he accredited the contentious lyrics to the influence of "black music" and argued that "the spirit of Hip-Hop should always be peace and love" (CGTN, 2018). He also took down the song from online music platforms and promised to make songs with more "positive energy" in the future. Before long, however, PG One's prospects of releasing any songs for the mainland Chinese audience became entirely impossible. On January 19, 2018, a media conference was reportedly held by the State Administration of Press, Publication, Radio, Film, and Television (SAPPRFT) of China at the time, addressing the issue of the recent popularity of various subcultures, including Hip-Hop. Gao Changli, the dean of the propaganda office at SAPPRFT, stated at the conference that media programs should absolutely not use artists "whose heart is not aligned with the Party and whose morality is not noble" and also specifically required that "programs should not feature artists with tattoos [or depict] Hip-Hop culture, subculture (non-mainstream culture) and dispirited culture (decadent culture)" (Quackenbush and Chen, 2018). Right after the announcement, all of PG One's works were pulled down from Chinese online music platforms, and he has not been able to release a single song in mainland China since then. Other Hip-Hop musicians faced similar hardships as some of their works were criticized and pulled down either by the platforms or by the artists themselves. Nonetheless, the second season of The Rap of China was aired on July 14, 2018, much to everyone's surprise. Without the state making any announcements regarding the status of the censorship, public performances of Hip-Hop music seemed to be allowed again and hence have recovered since then.

Thus, the story of Hip-Hop censorship in China can be roughly divided by three events into four time periods in 2017 and 2018, which become the focus of this study: the gradual elevation in mainstream media before the first season of The Rap of China (Period I); a quick rise in national fame and profitability afterward (Period II); a period of silence of the music scene after the censorship (Period III); and a return to publicity after the second season of The Rap of China (Period IV). My fieldwork in the summer of 2019 in five Chinese cities also validates the existence of these four periods (see Appendix A). It is also worth pointing out that there was no similar censorship of other music genres taking place in 2017 and 2018 in China.

There are two aspects in this story of Hip-Hop censorship worth pointing out. First, the exact target of the censorship has never been made clear by the state. Other than the ambiguous instructions given in the press conference that Hip-Hop was the target, little information was provided to the public regarding who exactly would be regulated and how the censorship would be implemented, leaving musicians in speculation. This makes the case an ideal site for the inquiry of the political impact on genre as the genre boundary is being reshaped in the process of this political intervention. Second, China's socialist market economy is characterized by the deeply intertwined economic and political field (Tong and Hung, 2012). A market economy notwithstanding, the state is actively participating in the market, rewarding (or punishing) politically subjugated (or nonconformed) entities through political or economic interventions (Baranovitch, 2003;Tong and Hung, 2012). As a result, obedience to political order is usually the premise of economic return. This demonstrates why becoming "popular" also implies becoming "politically tamed" in the context of China, which suggests that the dispersion model would be a valid explanation of the political impact on genres as problematic content and styles are supposed to be suppressed in exchange for popularity and profitability.

paragraphs: To test the dispersion model and examine how the Hip-Hop censorship affected music genres in China, I collected an original dataset of 53,364 songs from Chinesemusic.com, one of the most popular online music platforms in mainland China. 4 The platform has around 30 million monthly active users (MAU) as of 2019 and is well-known for incorporating works from both big-time musicians and independent artists. It also has a high reputation for a detailed classification of genres based on the industry standard. The songs are collected from all studio albums of four genres, namely Pop, Hip-Hop, Rock, and Folk, released between January 1, 2015 and December 31, 2018, where each song is assigned to one particular genre exclusively as its primary genre by its uploaders, usually the musicians or their production team. The main focus of this study are the songs released in 2017 and 2018, with the songs released in the previous two years used for training genre classifiers (which will be elaborated on in the next section).

The songs I collected are all Chinese Language Songs [华语歌曲], which means that they are made or sung by Chinese-speaking artists and are mostly targeting a Chinese-speaking audience in mainland China. I collected basic information of each song including its song name, genre, artist(s), and times being played on the platform. I also collected the information of the album to which the song is affiliated, including the album name and its release date. I managed to collect the lyrics of all the songs unless they were not available on the platform either because the songs are purely instrumental or the lyrics were not uploaded to the platform; in the latter case, I searched the lyrics from other sources to collect as many of them as possible. I also collected the audio files of all the songs that were accessible on the platform either for free or with compensation (see more details in Appendix B.1).

Table 1 shows the distribution of the songs in terms of their release year and their genre. As the table suggests, the number of Pop songs released every year are much higher than the other three genres, suggesting that the other three target relatively nuanced markets. With that said, Hip-Hop songs skyrocketed in 2017 and again in 2018, the increased rate of which outcompetes Rock and Folk songs, suggesting that the genre is trending. Based on the aforementioned fact that the Hip-Hop scene is more closely associated with Rock in China historically, I contend that Rock is a relatively close genre to Hip-Hop, whereas Pop also shares such closeness in response to the prevalence of Hip-Hop trending starting from 2017. Folk, on the other hand, is a distanced genre compared to Hip-Hop and thus serves as a reference category for the analysis.

To test the dispersion model, I closely examine how the formal elements of these genres are impacted by the censorship, and how the censorship effect varies across and within the different genres. The formal elements of a song in the popular music industry typically include its sound 5 and lyrics, which together make up the key components of a song (Frith 2017). Due to the different nature of sound and language, I consider slightly different forms of the dispersion effect for each.

Based on the implications of the dispersion model, I expect that the post-censorship Hip-Hop songs will sound different from the precensorship Hip-Hop songs, and this would be more salient among songs that are widely distributed than those that are not. Hence: H1a. As for the targeted genre (Hip-Hop), the songs released after the censorship sound significantly less similar to those before.
H1b. Within the censored genre (Hip Hop), such a difference is more salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.

On the other hand, genres that are stylistically close to the censored genre will take up elements of the style of the censored genre under the dispersion model. Yet by virtue of being a different genre, which is supposed to be consistent with its own genre conventions, the widely distributed works of the closely related genres will also be less likely to take up the style of the censored genre. Hence: H2a. As for the genre that is close to the censored genre (Rock), the songs released after the censorship, compared to those before, sound significantly more similar to the songs of the censored genre (Hip-Hop).
H2b. Within the close genre (Rock), this convergence is less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.

Since Pop is a special category that reflects the trending genres, I expect that Pop songs will have adopted some stylistic traits of Hip-Hop when the latter became viral in 2017. Subsequently, I would expect Pop to start dropping Hip-Hop style after the censorship because censorship implies that Hip-Hop would be less likely to be trending as before. Similarly, I expect that widely distributed Pop 4 Chinesemusic.com is a pseudonym for copyright protective reasons. I collected the entirety of the data in September 2019, and thus the total number of released songs in the four years of interest remains stable pinned at that collection period. 5 While in common parlance the sonic aspect of a song is often simply referred to as the "music", in order to avoid being confused with other meanings of the word "music" (e.g., "popular music"), I will use the term "sound" in this article to mean the sonic aspect of a song.
K. Nie songs will be comparatively less affected by the censorship as Hip-Hop is not the only trending genre.

H3a. As for the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the songs released after the censorship, compared to those before, sound significantly less similar to the songs of the censored trending genre (Hip-Hop).
H3b. Within the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), this divergence is less salient among high-profile songs than low profile-songs.

Finally, distanced genres will generally be little affected by the censorship. For this study, Folk is used as a distant genre for comparison. Hence: H4. As for the genre that is distanced from the censored genre (Folk), songs released after the censorship, compared to those before, will have little change in their musical similarity to the songs of the censored genre (Hip-Hop).

As for the lyrics, I expect that post-censorship Hip-Hop songs will write about sensitive topics (such as sex, violence, drugs, or politics) significantly less than pre-censorship ones. Hence: H5a. As for the targeted genre (Hip-Hop), the songs released after the censorship will write significantly less about sensitive topics.
H5b. Within the censored genre (Hip Hop), such a difference is more salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.

I expect similar patterns to emerge in terms of Hip-Hop's censorship impacting the lyrics of other genres, with the hypotheses regarding lyrics paralleling the hypotheses regarding sound of the musical genres examined. Hence: H6a. As for the genre that is close to the censored genre (Rock), the songs released after the censorship will write significantly more about sensitive topics.
H6b. Within the close genre (Rock), the increase in lyrics about sensitive topics will be less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.
H7a. As for the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the songs released after the censorship will write significantly less about sensitive topics.

H7b. Within the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the decrease in lyrics about sensitive topics will be less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs (as the high-profile songs would not incorporate more sensitive topics in the first place).

Yet by virtue of being a different genre, which is supposed to be consistent with its own genre conventions, the widely distributed works of the closely related genres will also be less likely to take up the style of the censored genre. Hence: H2a. As for the genre that is close to the censored genre (Rock), the songs released after the censorship, compared to those before, sound significantly more similar to the songs of the censored genre (Hip-Hop).

H2b. Within the close genre (Rock), this convergence is less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.

Since Pop is a special category that reflects the trending genres, I expect that Pop songs will have adopted some stylistic traits of Hip-Hop when the latter became viral in 2017. Subsequently, I would expect Pop to start dropping Hip-Hop style after the censorship because censorship implies that Hip-Hop would be less likely to be trending as before. Similarly, I expect that widely distributed Pop 4 Chinesemusic.com is a pseudonym for copyright protective reasons. I collected the entirety of the data in September 2019, and thus the total number of released songs in the four years of interest remains stable pinned at that collection period. 5 While in common parlance the sonic aspect of a song is often simply referred to as the "music", in order to avoid being confused with other meanings of the word "music" (e.g., "popular music"), I will use the term "sound" in this article to mean the sonic aspect of a song.

K. Nie songs will be comparatively less affected by the censorship as Hip-Hop is not the only trending genre.

H3a. As for the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the songs released after the censorship, compared to those before, sound significantly less similar to the songs of the censored trending genre (Hip-Hop).

H3b. Within the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), this divergence is less salient among high-profile songs than low profile-songs.

Finally, distanced genres will generally be little affected by the censorship. For this study, Folk is used as a distant genre for comparison. Hence: H4. As for the genre that is distanced from the censored genre (Folk), songs released after the censorship, compared to those before, will have little change in their musical similarity to the songs of the censored genre (Hip-Hop).

As for the lyrics, I expect that post-censorship Hip-Hop songs will write about sensitive topics (such as sex, violence, drugs, or politics) significantly less than pre-censorship ones. Hence: H5a. As for the targeted genre (Hip-Hop), the songs released after the censorship will write significantly less about sensitive topics.

H5b. Within the censored genre (Hip Hop), such a difference is more salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.

I expect similar patterns to emerge in terms of Hip-Hop's censorship impacting the lyrics of other genres, with the hypotheses regarding lyrics paralleling the hypotheses regarding sound of the musical genres examined. Hence: H6a. As for the genre that is close to the censored genre (Rock), the songs released after the censorship will write significantly more about sensitive topics.

H6b. Within the close genre (Rock), the increase in lyrics about sensitive topics will be less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs.

H7a. As for the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the songs released after the censorship will write significantly less about sensitive topics.

H7b. Within the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the decrease in lyrics about sensitive topics will be less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs (as the high-profile songs would not incorporate more sensitive topics in the first place).
sections.preview_head: To test the dispersion model and examine how the Hip-Hop censorship affected music genres in China, I collected an original dataset of 53,364 songs from Chinesemusic.com, one of the most popular online music platforms in mainland China. 4 The platform has around 30 million monthly active users (MAU) as of 2019 and is well-known for incorporating works from both big-time musicians and independent artists. It also has a high reputation for a detailed classification of genres based on the industry standard. The songs are collected from all studio albums of four genres, namely Pop, Hip-Hop, Rock, and Folk, released between January 1, 2015 and December 31, 2018, where each song is assigned to one particular genre exclusively as its primary genre by its uploaders, usually the musicians or their production team.
sections.preview_tail: H7b. Within the category that represents the trending genres (Pop), the decrease in lyrics about sensitive topics will be less salient among high-profile songs than low-profile songs (as the high-profile songs would not incorporate more sensitive topics in the first place).

paragraphs: As for the genre that is distanced from the censored genre (Folk), the songs released after the censorship will have little change in their way of engaging with sensitive topics.

One limitation of the primary dataset collected on Chinesemusic.com is that there is no pre-censorship data. The data was collected in January 2019 and hence the censored data, i.e. songs released before the censorship and pulled down after the censorship, could not be collected on the platform directly. To supplement the dataset and validate the results, I collected a small sample of the censored Hip-Hop songs (see details in Appendix B.2). For each censored song, I used various sources to collect and verify its basic information, and I eventually managed to collect the lyrics and the audio files of 104 songs. These songs are used to train a censorship classifier which measures how similar a given song is compared to the censored songs acoustically. Moreover, the additional dataset of censored songs, which are all of the targeted genre (Hip-Hop), supplement and enhance the collection of Hip-Hop songs from the main dataset.

For the objective of this study, I follow the latter approach and use computational tools widely used in the field of Music Information Retrieval (MIR) to construct my measurements.

MIR is a rapidly developing field and is widely applied in the music industry primarily to develop recommendation systems for music platforms by detecting acoustic similarities between songs (Song, Dixon, and Pearce, 2012). MIR algorithms usually start by processing digital signals of the audio file of a song and measures the patterns of the signals based on various types of acoustic measurements. The musical qualities of the song are thus translated into a series of acoustic measurements whose values are idiosyncratic. Songs that sound similar should have similar values for these measurements. Utilizing this technology, I constructed a Hip-Hop classifier by processing all the songs released in 2015/2016 for their acoustic measurements and training a neural network based on the different patterns in these measurements between Hip-Hop and non-Hip-Hop songs. I then use this classifier to predict the probability of every song released in 2017/2018 to be classified as Hip-Hop. The probability score, which I call here Hip-Hoppiness, is the key dependent variable in this study. I also similarly constructed a censorship classifier with a sample of censored and uncensored songs as the training set to assign the probability score of a given song being classified as a censored song, or Censorshipness. The whole procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1, exemplified by the construction of Hip-Hoppiness (see details in Appendix C.1), where SP stands for signal processing and NN stands for neural network.

I use interrupted time series models (Bernal, Cummins, and Gasparrini,;McDowall, McCleary, and Bartos, 2019;Wagner et al., 2002) to measure the impact of each of the three interventions mentioned in the background section: the broadcasting of the first season of The Rap of China, the censorship, and the broadcasting of the second season of The Rap of China. Interrupted time series models are the best way of modeling for this study as they are useful for measuring the change of the outcomes across time due to exogenous interventions. ) will make an impact on the level of Hip-Hoppiness instantaneously (β 2g , β 4g , β 6g ) and affect the trend of Hip-Hoppiness in the subsequent period (β 3g , β 5g , β 7g ). I also use the same model for regressing Censorshipness.

As for the lyrical content, I use both topic models as well as a dictionary approach to measure the change of ways in which lyrics are written. This is because there are two aspects that are usually concerned by the regulators: topics and words. In China, topics that are explicitly related to sex, violence, deviant behavior (such as smoking marijuana), and also politics (especially anti-state messages) are all sensitive topics that are usually banned from distributing publicly, as demonstrated in the censorship of rock music (Baranovitch, 2003). Hence, these topics are the main focus of this component of the analysis. I additionally include the investigation of topics related to personal struggles, which is also a popular topic among Hip-Hop musicians in reaction to censorship (Lena, 2006). Moreover, sensitive words, including swear words and those that are explicitly associated with the aforementioned sensitive topics (e.g., marijuana), are also highly sensitive to cultural regulators. Specifically, some of these terms may trigger instant censorship by the algorithms of music platforms to avoid getting into trouble with cultural regulators. Consequently, musicians usually re-write these terms in a veiled but still recognizable way.

Therefore, it is interesting to see, first, how topics of the lyrics may have changed after the censorship, and second, how the use of sensitive terms may have changed as well. To test the change of topics, I use topic models (Mohr and Bogdanov, 2013) as have already been widely used in social sciences in dealing with a high volume of texts (e.g., DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei, 2013;Evans and Aceves, 2016;Roberts, 2018). Topic models prove to be useful for identifying and understanding the themes and topics of a large corpus of texts. Here, I use the Structural Topic Model (STM), which brings document-level information (in this case, information of the song) into modeling (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley, 2014). It allows me to measure how document-level covariates affect topical prevalence, referring to the proportion of the document devoted to a given topic. In other words, with STM I am able to measure how significantly the censorship changes the songs' engagement with certain topics. The specifics of the topic models that I use for the analysis here can be found in Appendix C.2.

To test changes in regard to the use of sensitive terms in lyrics, I use a dictionary approach. I developed a dictionary of sensitive terms by searching for all the covered terms in the dataset and reading the lyrics of the censored songs (see Appendix C.3). paragraphs: Fig. 2 shows the outcome of the change of Hip-Hoppiness and Censorshipness after applying the model to the songs of each genre (see details in Appendix C.2). The colored lines are the linear regressions of Hip-Hoppiness and Censorshipness on release date in each period for the corresponding genre. The parameter estimates of the regressions, as well as those of the subsequent regressions, can be found in the appendices. As the graphic on the left shows, 2017/2018 Hip-Hop songs typically have a score of Hip-Hoppiness between 0.6 and 0.8. The level of Hip-Hoppiness is generally fluctuating around 0.67 with a significant drop right after the censorship by 0.013. Hip-Hoppiness gradually increases during Period III and stays relatively static in Period IV. The results thus provide statistical support for H1a (Hip-Hop songs released after censorship sound less similar to those from before). The left graphic also verifies the hypothesis that Folk songs, as a distant genre, are generally unaffected by the interventions in the targeted genre (Hip-Hop). There is, however, a statistically significant surge in Hip-Hoppiness by 0.029 in the close genre of Rock and a drop by 0.012 in Pop after the censorship, indicating a dispersion effect of the censorship on genres other than Hip-Hop. This demonstrates that, right after the shock of the censorship, Pop songs were less likely to incorporate musical elements that are similar to Hip-Hop, while Rock songs were welcoming Hip-Hop elements. The findings thus provide statistical support for H2a, H3a, and H4.

Similar but slightly different patterns can be found for Censorshipness as supplementary evidence in Fig. 2. As the graphic on the right shows, Folk is generally unaffected by any of the interventions in terms of Censorshipness. Hip-Hop and Pop slide in their Censorshipness after the censorship by 0.013 and 0.012, respectively, whereas Rock increases its Censorshipness by 0.029. This indicates that the censorship had a negative impact on Hip-Hop and Pop songs in terms of incorporating musical elements that are similar to K. Nie

censored songs, while Rock incorporates more such elements after the censorship. Generally, the impact of censorship on the four genres is parallel with their change of Hip-Hoppiness although slightly less significant for Pop and Rock.

The analysis above demonstrates that following the censorship, at the genre-level, Hip-Hop songs and Pop songs become less like 2015/2016 Hip-Hop songs generally and the censored songs particularly, and Rock songs become more like them, while Folk songs remain generally untouched. However, as mentioned before, songs that are more widely distributed might have different patterns than those that are not since the former are more likely to be on the radar of censorship. To test if high-profile songs have a different pattern from low-profile songs, I break down each genre into a top group and a tail group, where I order the songs by their number of times being listened to on the platform and sample the top 10% most-listened to songs in each of the four periods into the top group and the last 50% into the tail group. This is an arbitrary choice but consistent with the fact that music distribution is highly skewed so only a small proportion of songs enjoy a high number of plays while most songs are relatively unheard (see details in Appendix B.1). The patterns in the top group and tail group will represent how censorship affected high-profile and low-profile songs differently. The difference between the two groups in terms of their patterns of change of Hip-Hoppiness and Censorshipness is shown in Fig. 3.

As illustrated in Fig. 3, patterns are different between the top groups and the tail groups. The primary finding is that the high-profile Hip-Hop songs dropped their Censorshipness but not so much their Hip-Hoppiness, whereas the low-profile Hip-Hop songs, on the other hand, dropped their Hip-Hoppiness but not so much their Censorshipness. This implies that the censored Hip-Hop songs have relatively distinctive style even among Hip-Hop songs, and this style is dispersed from the high-profile songs yet somehow preserved in the lowprofile songs. The other outstanding finding is that the change of Rock and Pop identified in Fig. 2 is generally driven by their respective tail groups rather than the top groups. The low-profile Pop songs became less similar to Hip-Hop songs as well as the censored songs, while the high-profile Pop songs were less significantly affected. This suggests that the low-profile Pop songs, in parallel with Hip-Hop, were dispersed from engaging with the Hip-Hop style, and the general steadiness of the high-profile Pop songs may just be because Pop is a mixture of trending genres and thus only a small fraction of the songs related to Hip-Hop style may be affected by the censorship. that the shifts in musical qualities are caused by the censorship rather than the outcome of "intrinsic" development. Moreover, Appendix F, in which I take a close look at the songs produced by specific musicians whose songs were banned by the censorship, also validates the findings above.

paragraphs: Fig. 4 presents the results of censorship's effect on some of the topics identified by the algorithms among songs released in Period II and Period III across genres and by their popularity (see details in Appendix C.3). As the graphic suggests, Hip-Hop songs in fact engage more with topics related to sexual themes after the censorship, especially among high-profile songs. Low-profile Hip-Hop songs also engage more with stories of personal struggles, which coincide with how their American counterparts reacted to censorship (Lena, 2006). On the other hand, high-profile Hip-Hop songs touch less on topics related to deviant behavior (such as smoking and drinking), while low-profile Hip-Hop songs write less about violence after the censorship. Therefore, H5a (expectation that Hip-Hop songs released after censorship will write significantly less about sensitive topics) can be generally supported (with the exception for the sex topics), although H5b (salience by distribution level) is not explicitly supported due to the ambiguity of the pattern of change. Other genres are generally less affected except low-profile Rock songs also became more engaged with struggling stories, which shows a similar pattern with how they change musically. Therefore, H6a, H6b, H7a, H7b (regarding expected changes to Rock and Pop lyrics) are generally rejected, while H8 (Folk relatively unaffected) remains true for the sample. The results suggest that the censorship has primarily affected the topics of the lyrics in Hip-Hop music but not so much in other genres.

While the results suggest that the censorship dispersed some sensitive topics from Hip-Hop, it is perhaps surprising to see that the topic related to sex increases in Hip-Hop lyrics after the censorship. This is perhaps because the frequently used terms in the topic related to sex may sometimes be used not to indicate specific sexual behaviors but rather as swear words to express anger, disappointment, or simply to demonstrate the "authenticity" of the lyricist (Zhang, 2020). Moreover, to circumvent the censorship, these terms may be rewritten into a covert form that cannot be easily detected by the censors or the platform screening algorithms. K. Nie

Therefore, I speculate that the increasing significance of sexual words in Hip-Hop songs corresponds with the increasing use of covering strategies but does not indicate an increase in the lyrics' active engagement with themes of sexual behaviors. To test the speculation, I picked out all the songs that contain sensitive terms explicitly related to sex, violence, deviant behaviors, and politics, among which I identified the proportion of songs that use sex terms. I calculated the number of songs that use the sex terms but re-write them in a covert form, and I also calculated the number of songs that use the sex terms that could only refer to sexual behaviors instead of swear-words (see details in Appendix C.3). This results in the following two graphics that substantiate my speculation. Fig. 5 shows that, after the censorship, there was an increasing number of songs with the sex terms in both the top and tail groups of the Hip-Hop genre along with an increasing number of songs that re-wrote these terms in a covert form. The pattern of change in the songs of the other three genres is not as evident as in the Hip-Hop songs. On the other hand, Fig. 6 demonstrates that among all the Hip-Hop songs using the sex terms, only a relatively small proportion of the songs use the terms that could only refer to sexual behaviors. The two graphics, along with Fig. 4, validate the dispersion model by suggesting that the sensitive topics were dispersed from being a salient feature of Hip-Hop music and the sensitive terms were preserved only in a covert form.

sections.paper_id: Nie - 2021 - Disperse and preserve the perverse computing how hip-hop censorship changed popular music genres in
paragraphs: This paper offers a novel insight for the study of genres by investigating the impact of political interventions on genre boundaries. By examining how Hip-Hop censorship in China changed musical forms across various genres, I found that the censorship significantly impacted how music sounds and what the lyrics are about. Hip-Hop songs released after the censorship, in general, sound less like those before, but the level of change varies between high-profile and low-profile songs with the former becoming significantly different from the songs that were censored and the latter less so. Other musical genres were affected too, the degree of which depends on their artistic affinity to Hip-Hop. While Folk, which is artistically distanced from Hip-Hop, was largely unaffected, Rock songs, especially those that are low-profile, become more "Hip-Hoppy" after the censorship perhaps due to its affinity with Hip-Hop. Pop songs also drop their Hip-Hoppiness after the censorship generally and the decrease is most significant among low-profile songs. On the one hand, this may suggest that Pop, as partly reflecting the prevailing genre of Hip-Hop, made a response in parallel with Hip-Hop to avoid being K. Nie

similarly targeted by the censorship. On the other hand, this also suggests the versatility of Pop to be able to incorporate various musical styles, and thus also easily abandon cultural forms that are risky to their profitability.

I also found that the censorship changed what Hip-Hop musicians write in their lyrics. Following censorship, Hip-Hop lyrics engage less with topics related to violence and deviant behavior (such as drug use) but include more references to sex, which is perhaps easier to be written in a veiled form while maintaining Hip-Hop's authenticity and, in any case, many of the sexual terms are not necessarily related to sexual conducts per se. The findings clearly depict how political intervention disperses sensitive elements from the politically problematic genre, which are nevertheless preserved in a less salient way by the limitedly distributed works of that genre and by other adjacent genres. The dispersion and preservation that happened in other genres also took place mostly among their low-profile works, indicating that although genre boundaries may fluctuate due to political intervention, it does not significantly involve high-profile works, which define the genre conventions that are not easily mutable and which themselves are more likely to be under government scrutiny than low-profile works.

This study makes solid scholarly contributions in at least four ways. First, it fills the previous gap in the study of genres by expanding the focus of the existing literature from the impact of economic profitability and artistic appraisal on reshaping genre boundaries to also include the effects of political intervention. Particularly, the study proposes the dispersion model, characterizing a forced adjustment between genres with regard to their boundaries. The model can be further used and tested for other empirical cases of political intervention in not only authoritarian but also democratic nations, as political interventions and cultural censorship occur in both societies. Additionally, the dispersion model can also be tested for other cases that involve a forced adjustment between genres. For example, the "Production of Culture" perspective suggests that cultural production can be shaped and restructured by "law and regulation" (Peterson and Anand, 2004). While political interventions may fall into the aspect of "law and regulation", there are many additional types of laws and regulations that may not be apparently political but still may conduce exogenous shock on genres. Examples would include the (de-)regulation of the radio industry impacting the number of radio formats, which are comprised of closely related sub-genres of music aimed at a specific demographic (Lee, 2004). Copyright protections, or a lack thereof, may also have a dispersing effect by causing cultural producers to avoid certain genres due to the lack of market competence in protecting against the rampant pirated works within that genre (Griswold, 1981). Further research can explore whether the dispersion model holds in other cases of law and regulation or other aspects suggested by the "Production of Culture" perspective that may affect cultural production, such as technological developments, industry structures, and so on.

Second, this study provides new perspective into thinking about genre boundaries by pointing out the importance of examining the formal elements of the works associated with the genres in question. Consistent with the findings of previous studies that show artistic genres are not inherently yoked with stationary stylistic conventions (Lena, 2004(Lena, , 2012)), this study corroborates and enhances this argument with rich evidence at the level of the formal elements of songs themselves. This new evidence demonstrates that not only can genres be used to make distinctions between similar types of music for social and economic purposes (Lena, 2012;Roy, 2004), but also that the songs classified into the same genre can be remarkably different in their form across time. In other words, making music more "Hip-Hoppy" doesn't necessarily make the musician a "Hip-Hop musician". Rather, the genre label attached to the musician still depends on how they identify themselves and in which genre field they think they are struggling in. They may strategically shift their style of music while not changing their artistic identity to serve their interests, whether economic, artistic, or political. Or, they may sometimes keep their musical style while changing the genre they want to identify themselves with. Therefore, future research on the dynamics between genres and the formal elements of the works associated with the genres might yield additional promising findings down the road.

Third, adding to the literature on the impact of state censorship on dissidents or challenging actions in authoritarian societies (Esberg, 2020;King, Pan, and Roberts, 2013;Lorentzen, 2014;Roberts, 2018), the study here suggests a more nuanced effect of censorship in the cultural domain when the target of the censorship is difficult to define. The story here, on the one hand, resonates with the idea of "the weapons of the weak" (Scott, 1989) in that artists manage to resist the imposition of political interference in their creative practices. By virtue of working around the censorship with domain knowledge, the artists equip themselves with the power embedded in their everyday production that allows them to fight implicitly against state hegemony. On the other hand, though, this does not imply that political interventions are not effective. In fact, it might be exactly what the authoritarian state expects from its intervention: as long as the subcultures are not challenging the mainstream culture, they will be fine (Tong and Hung, 2012). Further research might benefit from investigating various kinds of censorship, including blacklists where the targets of censorship have been clearly specified, to see if the "dispersion" effect would vary depending on different forms of the regulated objects.

Finally, this study also contributes to the formal study of cultural forms in general by showcasing how computational tools, such as MIR algorithms and topic models, are helpful in constructing formal measurements and understanding the change and development of culture. The computational devices provide handy tools for measuring the qualities and patterns of the cultural form more directly. Compared to the proxies used in earlier studies, the computational tools help overcome the risk of overestimation or deviation of the results when the proxies cannot accurately reflect the artistic traits of the works (Mohr et al., 2020). They also help process a large amount of audio and textual data in a relatively reliable and consistent way, which makes a detailed investigation of the cultural form's changes at an aggregate level possible. Yet, computational tools also suffer from their own disadvantages. For example, computational tools cannot substitute human beings in interpreting the meaning of semantic structures (Mohr et al., 2020). Also, computational tools usually cannot provide answers to how the change of cultural forms is actually made in artistic practices, which is very critical to the understanding of the change of cultural forms. Therefore, as endeavored in the present study that draws evidence from both quantitative analysis and qualitative data (see Appendix A), future studies would benefit from synthesizing the findings of both computational and qualitative research to map out the patterns of how cultural production is shaped by social conditions.
